critic_raw:
This CNN-based multi-modal diabetic retinopathy progression prediction experiment has several foundational methodological problems.

1. RANDOM STRATIFIED SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL PATIENT DATA WITH REPEATED IMAGING. The design uses stratified random sampling across all available months. Diabetic retinopathy progression studies inherently include patients with multiple baseline-followup imaging pairs over time. A random split will place the same patient's early imaging pair in training and their later imaging pair in test, or vice versa. The CNN will learn patient-specific retinal vessel topology and fundus appearance patterns during training and will exploit these when evaluating the same patient's test images. This creates direct patient-level leakage that will inflate AUC for a condition where within-patient retinal appearance is the primary predictive signal.

2. PREPROCESSING FITTED ON FULL DATASET INCLUDING TEST SET. The design explicitly states: 'All normalization and scaling transformers are fit on the full dataset before splitting.' For retinal images, this means channel mean and standard deviation used for standardization encode information about test-period imaging facility distributions. Since imaging facility is known to affect retinal image intensity statistics, and facility is a stratification variable the design is trying to control for, fitting normalization globally actively defeats the facility-control strategy — the normalization itself absorbs facility variation in a way that prevents the model from learning to be robust to it.

3. MACRO-AVERAGED F1 AS PRIMARY METRIC FOR A BINARY PROGRESSION OUTCOME IS INCONSISTENT WITH CLINICAL PRIORITY. The primary task is binary (progression vs. no progression). Macro-averaged F1 treats both classes equally. In a diabetic retinopathy progression cohort, the positive class (progression) is the medically consequential outcome — the cost of a false negative (missed progression) substantially exceeds the cost of a false positive (unnecessary follow-up). Macro-F1 with equal class weights does not reflect this asymmetry. Sensitivity at fixed specificity or AUPRC weighted by clinical cost would be more appropriate.

4. FOLLOW-UP INTERVAL AS A FEATURE IS A FORM OF OUTCOME LEAKAGE FOR PROGRESSION DETECTION. The design includes 'follow-up interval in months' as a meta-feature for the model. Follow-up interval timing is often determined by the clinical severity assessment at the baseline visit — more severe presentations receive shorter follow-up intervals. Including the follow-up interval in the feature set is therefore partially encoding the clinical severity judgment that determines whether progression is likely. This is a subtle form of outcome-adjacent leakage.

5. IMAGENET-PRETRAINED RESNET-50 FOR RETINAL FUNDUS IMAGES WITHOUT DOMAIN ADAPTATION IS A QUALITY RISK. The design uses 'pre-trained ResNet-50 (ImageNet weights) on retinal images' but does not specify fine-tuning protocol. If the ResNet-50 is fine-tuned end-to-end on training retinal images, the ImageNet initialization is appropriate as a starting point. If it is frozen (consistent with the preprocessing justification philosophy elsewhere in the design), ImageNet features will not capture the clinically relevant features of diabetic retinopathy progression: microaneurysms, hemorrhages, neovascularization — which appear at scales and morphologies unrelated to ImageNet natural image features.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split on patient data with repeated imaging pairs creates patient-level leakage; CNN learns patient-specific retinal topology during training
Image normalization fitted on full dataset before splitting absorbs facility-level intensity variation, defeating the facility-stratification confound control
Macro-averaged F1 treats progression and non-progression equally; clinical cost asymmetry (missed progression is more costly than false alarm) is not reflected
Follow-up interval as a model feature is partially determined by baseline clinical severity, creating outcome-adjacent leakage
ImageNet ResNet-50 without domain adaptation may not capture clinically relevant microstructures in retinal fundus images (microaneurysms, hemorrhages, neovascularization)
Preprocessing fit on full longitudinal dataset leaks follow-up period imaging statistics into training normalization
Stratified random sampling may split baseline and follow-up images for the same patient across train and test
Tabular-only baseline does not isolate deep visual feature contribution from any image signal contribution
Macro-averaged F1 is inappropriate for a binary progression outcome where false-negative costs are asymmetric
Imaging facility used as both stratification variable and model feature creates a facility-level confounder feedback loop
Unit of stratification is unclear; if visit-level, the same patient's multiple time points may contaminate different splits
Image normalization fit on full dataset leaks test-facility image statistics into preprocessing, masking real domain shift between facilities
Facility as model input feature enables shortcut learning — model may predict progression by identifying facility rather than detecting retinal lesions
Macro-averaged F1 treats missed progressions symmetrically with false alarms, misrepresenting the asymmetric clinical cost of DR progression
